original_hypothesis.title: FeeCollectable.setFeeParameters Validates Old State Instead of New Input
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: setFeeParameters() checks `if (feeBips > BIPS)` instead of `if (_feeBips > BIPS)`. It validates the current state variable rather than the new input parameter. This allows setting feeBips to any uint16 value (up to 65535 = 655.35%) as long as the current value is valid. The first call always passes since initial feeBips is 0. Once set above BIPS, calculateFees() will cause underflow (userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount where feeAmount > amountIn) and subsequent setFeeParameters calls will permanently revert.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The variable name mismatch between `feeBips` (state) and `_feeBips` (parameter) is a classic copy-paste or naming error. The validation is demonstrably wrong - it protects the wrong variable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation checks `feeBips > BIPS` (state variable) instead of `_feeBips > BIPS` (input parameter)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (feeBips > BIPS) { revert ErrInvalidFeeBips(); } followed by feeBips = _feeBips;
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: feeBips (old state) is validated instead of _feeBips (new input parameter)

analysis_questions: Which contracts inherit FeeCollectable and are deployed?
What is the current feeBips value in deployed instances?
What is the impact on calculateFees if feeBips > BIPS?
validation_requirements: Call setFeeParameters with _feeBips > 10000 when current feeBips <= 10000
Verify the call succeeds and feeBips is set to the invalid value
Test calculateFees with feeBips > 10000 to confirm underflow/DoS

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Fee operator calls setFeeParameters(collector, 20000). Since current feeBips is 0, the check (0 > 10000) is false, allowing the set. Now feeBips=20000 (200%). calculateFees computes feeAmount = amountIn * 20000 / 10000 = 2*amountIn, causing underflow in userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount. Any subsequent setFeeParameters call also reverts (20000 > 10000), permanently locking the broken fee.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS of fee-related functionality; potential fund extraction if fee > 100% is processable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of contracts inheriting FeeCollectable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Irrecoverable once set - no function can fix feeBips since validation blocks all future calls

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test validates input parameter vs state variable in setFeeParameters

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by reading feeBips state variable

validation_result.mechanism: State variable validated instead of input parameter
validation_result.impact_summary: Allows setting feeBips > 100%, causing underflow in calculateFees and permanently locking the fee parameters.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The function `setFeeParameters` in `FeeCollectable.sol` is intended to allow an authorized fee operator to update the fee collector address and the fee percentage (`_feeBips`). However, the validation check `if (feeBips > BIPS)` evaluates the existing state variable `feeBips` rather than the new input parameter `_feeBips`. This means that as long as the current fee is valid (e.g., its initial value of 0), the operator can set `_feeBips` to any `uint16` value, including values greater than `BIPS` (10,000, representing 100%). Once `feeBips` is set to a value greater than 10,000, any subsequent calls to `setFeeParameters` will revert because the old state is now invalid, permanently locking the invalid fee. Furthermore, any call to `calculateFees` will compute a `feeAmount` greater than `amountIn`, causing the subtraction `userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount` to underflow and revert, leading to a Denial of Service for any functionality relying on it.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows `if (feeBips > BIPS)` instead of `if (_feeBips > BIPS)`. This is a definitive logic error.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The validation checks the state variable `feeBips` instead of the input parameter `_feeBips`. This allows `_feeBips` to be set to a value greater than `BIPS`.
If `feeBips` is greater than `BIPS`, `feeAmount` will be greater than `amountIn`, causing the subtraction to underflow and revert.

calling_sequence: Subsequent calls to `setFeeParameters` revert because `20000 > 10000` is true
Calls to `calculateFees(amount)` revert due to underflow when `feeAmount > amount`
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is self-contained within `setFeeParameters` and `calculateFees` in `FeeCollectable.sol`.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A malicious or compromised fee operator can call `setFeeParameters` with a `_feeBips` value greater than 10,000. Because the function checks the old state, the transaction succeeds. The fee is now set to > 100%. Any future attempts to fix the fee by calling `setFeeParameters` will revert because the old state is now > 10,000. Any operations in inheriting contracts that call `calculateFees` will revert due to underflow, causing a permanent DoS.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH. Allows an operator to permanently brick any contract inheriting `FeeCollectable` that relies on `calculateFees`, or extract excessive fees if the underflow is somehow bypassed (though Solidity 0.8+ reverts on underflow). The fact that it permanently locks the fee parameters makes it unrecoverable.
recommended_mitigations: Change the validation in `setFeeParameters` to check the input parameter: `if (_feeBips > BIPS) revert ErrInvalidFeeBips();`
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy a mock contract inheriting `FeeCollectable`. Call `setFeeParameters(address(this), 20000)`. Verify it succeeds. Call `setFeeParameters(address(this), 1000)` and verify it reverts. Call `calculateFees(100)` and verify it reverts with an underflow error.
uncertainties: None of the provided contracts in the scope directly inherit `FeeCollectable`, so the exact impact depends on out-of-scope or future contracts that use this mixin.
